summarization.a: The chart displays the Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) for various reactions of molybdenum (Mo) with different oxidants at different temperatures.

data_extraction.a:
| Reaction                                      | ΔG Range (kJ/mol) | Trend with Temperature |
|----------------------------------------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| Mo + 3 H₂O(g) → MoO₃ + 3 H₂(g)             | ~0 to +100        | Slightly increases     |
| Mo + H₂O(g) → MoO₂ + H₂(g)                 | ~-200 to -100     | Increases              |
| Mo + O₂(g) → MoO₂                           | ~-600 to -500     | Increases              |
| Mo + 1.5 O₂(g) → MoO₃                       | ~-600 to -500     | Increases              |
| Mo + 2 N₂O(g) → MoO₂ + 2 N₂(g)             | ~-800 to -700     | Increases              |
| Mo + 2 O₃(g) → MoO₂ + O₂(g)                | ~-900 to -800     | Increases              |
| Mo + 3 N₂O(g) → MoO₃ + 3 N₂(g)             | ~-1000 to -900    | Increases              |
| Mo + 3 O₃(g) → MoO₃ + O₂(g)                | ~-1100 to -1000   | Increases              |

a.question: Why gibbs free energy calculations are made?
a.answer: Gibbs free energy changes of the oxidation reactions of molybdenum using H2O, N2O, O2, and O3 as oxidizing agents were calculated to understand the feasibility of ALE

a.question: Why only MoO2 and MoO3 are considered?
a.answer: MoO2 and MoO3 were considered, as they can form volatile chlorides and oxychlorides via deoxychlorination reactions with NbCl5. Oxides with lower oxidation states were also not available in the thermodynamic database.

a.question: Can H2O oxidize Mo to MoO3?
What about the oxidation of MoO2 to MoO3?
a.answer: The energy changes are negative over the whole temperature range, except for the reaction with H2O, which has a positive Gibbs free energy change in this temperature range.
